g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on E-Waste and Pollutants - a critical topic for sustainable engineering.
E-waste refers to discarded electrical or electronic devices at end-of-life, including computers, phones, and peripherals.
g.text: Examples: Computers, laptops, mobile phones, TVs, printers
Growing 30% annually in India - major environmental concern

g.data-narration: A computer contains 30% metals, 40% plastics, 15% organic compounds, and 15% trace elements - many toxic.
g.text: Contains 1000+ substances, many toxic: Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, Chromium

g.data-narration: Four major heavy metals pose severe health risks: Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, and Hexavalent Chromium.
g.text: Four Major Heavy Metals in E-Waste
These metals cause neurological damage, kidney failure, and cancer. Found in CRTs, batteries, circuit boards.

g.data-narration: Lead is the most prevalent pollutant in e-waste, found in CRTs and circuit boards, causing severe neurological damage.
Mercury is highly toxic, found in CFLs and switches, causing brain damage and neurological disorders.
Cadmium accumulates in the body, causing kidney damage, bone degeneration, and itai-itai disease.
Hexavalent Chromium is a known carcinogen used in metal coatings, causing respiratory problems and DNA damage.
Organic pollutants like PBDEs, PCBs, dioxins, and furans are persistent, bioaccumulate, and disrupt endocrine systems.
E-waste exposure causes severe health issues: respiratory problems, neurological damage, birth defects, and cancer.
E-waste contaminates soil, groundwater, and air through leaching, incineration, and bioaccumulation in food chains.
E-waste management follows hierarchy: prevention, reuse, recycling, energy recovery, and safe disposal.
Three key examples demonstrate real-world e-waste hazards: CRT lead leaching, CFL mercury release, and circuit board burning.
